frameworks.name: Rate Estimation Theory
frameworks.one_line: representational account: animals learn stimulus-distribution structure and derive contingency from it

frameworks.name: classical associative theory
frameworks.one_line: conditioning via temporal contiguity with responses tracking associative strength

frameworks.name: Rescorla-Wagner model
frameworks.one_line: delta-rule associative learning whose update structure is shown to approximate rate estimation

core_question: Can Rate Estimation Theory's computational and conceptual difficulties be repaired while preserving its representational core, and how does the fix relate to classical associative learning?
key_assumptions: Pavlovian conditioning is captured either by contiguity-based associations or by contingency derived from learned rate representations
Contingency requires comparing stimulus-conditional reinforcement rate to a background rate
Continuous time underlies stimulus-reinforcer relations rather than discrete trials
manipulations: Background reinforcement rate varied while stimulus-conditional rate is held constant (contingency without contiguity change)
key_findings: Rate Estimation Theory in its original form faces its own computational and conceptual problems
A revised formulation fixes these problems while retaining the theory's representational core
The repaired rate-estimation algorithm closely resembles the Rescorla-Wagner update rule
The substantive difference between the theories lies in the response rule rather than the learning rule
interpretations: The classical/representational dichotomy is narrower than assumed: shared learning dynamics can support either view depending on how responses are read out
Contingency-based explanations of background-rate manipulations can be preserved within an associative-style learning rule
